title: RAndomised controlled trial to imProve depressIon and the quality of life of people with Dementia using cognitive bias modification: RAPID study protocol
abstract: Introduction: Depressive symptoms are common and undermine the quality of life of people with Alzheimer's disease (AD). Cholinesterase inhibitors and antidepressants have all but no effect on the mood of patients, and their use increases adverse events. Cognitive bias modification (CBM) targets attentional and interpretative biases associated with anxiety, dysphoria and depression and may be useful to treat depression in AD (DAD). This trial aims to determine the effect of CBM on depression scores and the quality of life of people with DAD. Methods and analysis: Randomised, double-blind, parallel, controlled trial of CBM (1:1 allocation ratio). Participants will be 80 adults with probable AD living in the Western Australian community who score 8 or more on the Cornell Scale for Depression in Dementia (CSDD). They will have mild to moderate dementia (Mini-Mental State Examination-MMSE score ≥15) and will be free of severe sensory impairment or suicidal intent. The intervention will consist of 10 40 min sessions of CBM delivered over 2 weeks using a high-resolution monitor using a local computer station at the Western Australian Centre for Health and Ageing. The primary outcomes of interest are the 2week change, from baseline, in the severity of CSDD scores and the Quality of Life AD (QoL-AD) scores. Secondary outcomes include changes in the CSDD, QoL-AD after 12 weeks, and changes in MMSE scores, negative attentional and interpretative bias and the proportion of participants with CSDD <8 after 2 and 12 weeks. Zealand Clinical Trials Registry number ACTRN12614000420640, date registered 06/04/2014.
text: Introduction: Depressive symptoms are common and undermine the quality of life of people with Alzheimer's disease (AD). Cholinesterase inhibitors and antidepressants have all but no effect on the mood of patients, and their use increases adverse events. Cognitive bias modification (CBM) targets attentional and interpretative biases associated with anxiety, dysphoria and depression and may be useful to treat depression in AD (DAD). This trial aims to determine the effect of CBM on depression scores and the quality of life of people with DAD. Methods and analysis: Randomised, double-blind, parallel, controlled trial of CBM (1:1 allocation ratio). Participants will be 80 adults with probable AD living in the Western Australian community who score 8 or more on the Cornell Scale for Depression in Dementia (CSDD). They will have mild to moderate dementia (Mini-Mental State Examination-MMSE score ≥15) and will be free of severe sensory impairment or suicidal intent. The intervention will consist of 10 40 min sessions of CBM delivered over 2 weeks using a high-resolution monitor using a local computer station at the Western Australian Centre for Health and Ageing. The primary outcomes of interest are the 2week change, from baseline, in the severity of CSDD scores and the Quality of Life AD (QoL-AD) scores. Secondary outcomes include changes in the CSDD, QoL-AD after 12 weeks, and changes in MMSE scores, negative attentional and interpretative bias and the proportion of participants with CSDD <8 after 2 and 12 weeks. Zealand Clinical Trials Registry number ACTRN12614000420640, date registered 06/04/2014.
main_text: INTRODUCTION Dementia is a leading cause of disability, affecting as many as 5% of the population over the age of 65 and 40% of those older than 80 years. 1 2 As the World's population continues to age, the number of people living with dementia is expected to increase exponentially over the next 40 years, 3 and this will lead to growing demands on residential care and health services. 4 In addition, the characteristic cognitive decline that affects people with dementia is commonly accompanied by other mental health changes involving perception, reasoning, behaviour and mood. 5 Most relevant to the current proposal is the fact that about 25% of people with Alzheimer's disease (AD), which is the most frequent cause of dementia in Western societies, show evidence of clinically significant depressive symptoms at assessment independent of the level of severity of the disease. 5 6 Currently available data alsoStrengths and limitations of this study ▪ Strong empirical evidence supports the testing of cognitive bias modification as a treatment for depression in Alzheimer's disease. ▪ Cognitive bias modification interventions are not associated with clinically significant adverse events. ▪ The pragmatic definition of depression in this trial will be based on the use of a validated cutpoint on the Cornell Scale for Depression in Dementia rather than diagnostic criteria. ▪ The trial will be limited to people with depression in Alzheimer's disease of mild to moderate severity. ▪ The intervention will be limited to 2 weeks. show that symptoms of anxiety and depression tend to co-occur in AD and may affect as many as 1 in every 2 people during the course of the illness. 7 8 Worryingly, the presence of mood disturbances in AD increases disability and burden of care, 8 impairs quality of life 9 and has been associated with accelerated cognitive decline in longitudinal studies. 10 As currently available treatments for AD do not affect disease progression, 11 it is important that we develop interventions that improve the quality of life of these patients. Treatments for AD do not ameliorate psychological comorbidity Medications commonly used to treat people with AD have no obvious effect on mood. The cholinesterase inhibitors donepezil, galantamine and rivastigmine improve the cognitive scores of patients with AD compared with placebo, 12 but have no consistent impact on comorbid psychological and behavioural symptoms, including depression. 13 Similarly, data from randomised controlled trials suggest that memantine does not possess antidepressant properties. 14 Treatments for depression associated with AD do not work and cause more side effects A large multicentre trial of the antidepressants sertraline and mirtazapine for the treatment of depression associated with dementia failed to show any benefit of treatment. 15 The investigators randomly assigned 326 people with AD and depression to treatment with sertraline (n=107, up to 150 mg daily), mirtazapine (n=108, up to 45 mg daily) or placebo (n=111). Thirty-nine weeks of follow-up data were available. Treatment with antidepressants did not reduce depression scores relative to placebo after 13 or 39 weeks of treatment, but sertraline and mirtazapine were associated with greater frequency of adverse reactions than placebo (43%, 41% and 26%, respectively). Moreover, a systematic review of randomised placebo-controlled trials for the treatment of depression in dementia showed that the efficacy of antidepressants is equivalent to that of placebo over a period of 6-12 weeks. 16 These negative results are alarming, as depression is commonly associated with AD, hinders quality of life and is a significant source of stress for carers. 8 9 Novel and effective approaches to manage these patients are needed.The contribution of cognitive bias to depression Various psychological interventions have been tested for the treatment of depression until now, with the most robust empirical evidence for efficacy arising from trials of cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT). 17 CBT uses a systematic approach to cognitive restructuring, change of biased negative beliefs and behavioural activation to mitigate the intensity and presence of depression. 18 Meta-analysis of pooled data of published trials indicates that CBT may also contribute to decreasing the risk of relapse over 12-24 months, suggesting that its benefits might extend beyond the acute phase of treatment. 19 The theoretical framework underpinning CBT recognises that depressed mood becomes apparent when a set of biased beliefs and thinking processes predominate, commonly involving preferential attention to negative aspects of experience and an inflated tendency to impose negative interpretations to ambiguous events. Such biases in the processing of information initiate the cycle of negative thinking and behavioural changes that are characteristic of depression. CBT challenges negative thoughts about the self, others and the future with the aim of modifying biased ways of thinking and, consequently, leading to improved mood and behaviour. Effective shifts of dysfunctional biased beliefs have been associated with robust response to treatment and decreased risk of relapse of symptoms in cognitively intact people able to engage with the demands and requirements of the therapy. 20 However, a substantial gap persists in treatment options for depressed adults with cognitive impairment. Cognitive models of depression ascribe the development of symptoms to systematic biases in low-level mechanisms not readily accessible to conscious introspection that operate before thoughts are formed in ways that shape their nature. 21 Selective biases in attention and interpretation that favour the processing of emotionally negative information are believed to represent the psychological basis of disordered mood, and emerging empirical and clinical findings support this hypothesis. 22 People with depression have more difficulty keeping their attention away from negative stimuli than non-depressed people. For example, adults with depression show a series of faces on a computer screen selectively direct attention to sad faces, but show no such bias when presented with angry or happy faces. 23 In addition, when faced with ambiguity, people with depressed mood favour negative interpretations of stimuli. For example, when presented with the ambiguous cue word 'GROWTH' (which can be interpreted negatively to mean tumour or non-negatively to mean increased size or wealth), people with low mood are significantly faster to then complete fragments of semantically related words associated with the negative rather than the nonnegative meanings of the ambiguous cue: C_NC_ER and GR_AT_R (cancer and greater). 24 These findings invite the question: are these biases amenable to change, and could they be a target for the treatment of dysphoria and depression in people with dementia? What is cognitive bias modification? MacLeod and colleagues introduced the most widely used approach worldwide to modify attentional bias. 25 The procedure exposes participants to pairs of words or images on a computer screen for 500 ms, with each pair including one emotionally negative and one neutral item. Immediately after the words/images disappear, a single small visual probe is presented in the same spatial position where one of the original stimuli had been displayed. Participants are required to indicate, as quickly as possible, the orientation of this probe (horizontal or vertical), and their speed to accurately do so is recorded over dozens of trials. People who display an attentional bias to the more negative information are significantly faster to make this discrimination judgment for probes that appear in the same area as the negative stimuli compared with probes in the region of the neutral stimuli. Cognitive bias modification for attention (CBM-A) delivers hundreds of trials in which all probes are presented where the neutral rather than the negative stimuli had just appeared ( promoting attentional avoidance of negative information). A control condition presents probes with equal frequency to each of these two areas. In CBM to reduce negative interpretative bias (CBM-I), participants are exposed to ambiguous information, followed by a word fragment that must be completed in a semantically consistent manner. CBM-I delivers hundreds of trials in which fragments yield only words consistent with non-negative interpretations of the ambiguity (discouraging negative interpretation). Thus, the ambiguous text "When you chat to people at a party they are soon chuckling, because you are so," will be followed by the fragment W_T_Y (yielding WITTY as opposed to 'silly'). A control condition employs fragments that equally often yield words consistent with negative or non-negative interpretation of the ambiguity. Single sessions of CBM can reduce negative attentional and interpretive bias, although multiple sessions may be associated with more lasting change. 25 CBM improves mood: previous trial evidence Recent trial data show that CBM-A attenuates anxiety reactions to stressful life events, 26 reduces recurrent negative thought intrusions in chronic worriers, 27 decreases avoidant behaviours 28 and mitigates the intensity of depressive and dysphoric symptoms over a 2-week period. 29 CBM is also effective at mitigating depressive symptoms in people with major depressive disorder. Williams et al 30 randomised adults to CBM (n=38) or to a wait-list (n=31). CBM sessions were delivered daily over the internet for 1 week, and were associated with greater reduction in the severity of depressive symptoms.Rationale for the proposed trial Depression commonly affects people with AD during the course of their illness. Data from randomised controlled trials show that treatment with cholinesterase inhibitors, memantine and antidepressants is ineffective at reducing the severity of depressive symptoms, while the use of traditional forms of psychotherapy is hindered by the cognitive deficits that characterise AD. As the pronounced impairment of explicit declarative learning (ie, episodic memory) associated with AD does not compromise the implicit learning that occurs when patients acquire cue-outcome associations, 31 CBM may be a particularly suitable for use in this population.Objectives This trial aims to determine the effect of CBM on depressive symptoms and the quality of life of adults with depression in AD (DAD) after 2 weeks of treatment. We hypothesise that participants treated with active compared with control CBM will experience greater improvement of depressive symptoms and quality of life scores after 2 weeks of treatment. We also anticipate that the improvements in depression and quality of life scores will be maintained for 12 weeks and that these changes will be associated with a relative reduction in negative attentional and interpretative biases.METHODSStudy setting This study has been designed as a single centre trial based at the Western Australian Centre for Health & Ageing at the Royal Perth Hospital in Australia. Participants will be community-dwelling older adults with AD in contact with the metropolitan health services.Trial design The RAndomised controlled trial to imProve depressIon and the quality of life of people with Dementia (RAPID) is a parallel, double-blind, controlled randomised trial of CBM with a 1:1 allocation ratio.Eligibility criteria We will recruit 80 people with depression in mild to moderate severity AD according to the following inclusion criteria:Interventions Participants randomly assigned to the active and control CBM interventions will be exposed to the same study procedures and daily activities. They will be asked to attend the Western Australian Centre for Health and Ageing, Royal Perth Hospital, on a daily basis for a total of 10 CBM sessions (2 weeks-excluding weekends). Each session will be delivered on a 24″ high-resolution screen using a local PC station, and will last approximately 30 min: 15 min each for CBM-A and for CBM-I. In the CBM-A session, participants will be shown pairs of emotionally discrepant photos for 500 ms (sad or neutral/happy faces). Each pair will then be replaced by a small probe (square or circle) appearing in the screen position previously occupied by one of the photos. Participants will be instructed to use a response box to indicate the shape of the small probe (by pressing the circle or square buttons of the response box). The probe will then disappear and will be replaced, after 1 s, by another pair that of photos to initiate the next trial. The time to discriminate probe identity will be recorded automatically. In the active CBM condition, designed to reduce attention to negative information, probes will always appear in the position of neutral/happy faces. In the control condition, probes will appear 50% of the time in the position of neutral/happy faces and 50% of the time in the position of the sad faces. In the interpretative CBM sessions (CBM-I), single ambiguous cue-words that permit a negative and benign interpretation (eg, HIT) will first appear in the top half of the screen for 1 s. Then two words will appear on the bottom half of the screen: one on the left and the other on the right hand side. This word-pair will consist of one target word that is semantically related either to the negative or benign meaning of the ambiguous cue-word (eg, SUCCESS or PUNISH), and one foil word that is unrelated to either meaning of the ambiguous cue-word (eg, CLOUD). Participants will be asked to identify which is the target word and use the response box to indicate whether the semantically-related word appeared on the left or right-hand side of the screen (by pressing the left or the right hand side button of the response box, respectively). The time required to accurately identify the target word will be recorded automatically. In the active CBM condition, designed to reduce negative interpretations of ambiguity, target words always will be associated with the benign meanings of the cue-words, discouraging their negative interpretation. In the control condition, 50% of the target words will be associated with the benign meaning and 50% with the negative meanings of the cue-words. A trained graduate research officer will supervise all CBM sessions, and participants will be randomly assigned to the active or control CBM groups. Previous studies (reviewed in the Introduction section) have shown that attentional and interpretative biases can be modified successfully using these procedures. In addition, there is evidence that exposure to daily CBM leads to extinction of negative bias within 2 weeks, 35 36 thereby guiding the duration of our trial for efficacy while minimising treatment burden.Outcomes This trial has two primary outcomes of interest: depression and quality of life. We will use the CSDD to establish the presence of clinically significant symptoms of depression (CSDD ≥8), measure changes in the severity of symptoms over 2 and 12 weeks, and to ascertain the remission of symptoms after 2 and 12 weeks (CSDD <8). The primary outcome of interest is the change, from baseline, in the severity of symptoms after 2 weeks of treatment. The CSDD is the most widely used instrument worldwide to assess depression in dementia. The 19-item scale is rated on a three-point score that ranges from absent to severe. The scale has robust psychometric properties, with good inter-rater reliability and internal consistency. 34 We are not aware of data describing minimal clinical important differences or minimal detectable changes for this scale. The CSDD is a rater-based assessment that takes about 10 min to complete. 34 The Quality of Life AD (QoL-AD) scale will be this study's measure of quality of life. 37 It consists of 13 items that assess behavioural competence, psychological status, physical functioning and interpersonal environment that is of relevance to older adults. Each item offers four answers that range from poor (1) to excellent (4), with the total possible score ranging from 13 to 52. Higher scores indicate better quality of life. The QoL-AD has the added advantage of offering patient and carer versions. The QoL-AD has robust psychometric properties. 37 Changes in QoL-AD scores from baseline represent another outcome of interest of this trial, and will be measured 2 ( primary endpoint) and 12 weeks (secondary endpoint) after the baseline assessment. Before the intervention starts, all participants will complete a baseline assessment of attentional and interpretative biases, using assessment variants of the procedures described for the intervention. In the attentional assessment, probes will appear 50% of the time in the position of neutral/happy faces and 50% of the time in the position of sad faces. Negative attentional bias will be indexed by degree of relative speeding to discriminate probes in the latter condition compared with the former. In the interpretative assessment, target words will be related to the benign meaning of the ambiguous cue word 50% of the time and to its negative meaning 50% of the time. Negative interpretative bias will be indexed by degree of relative speeding to identify target words in the latter condition compared to the former. This assessment will be repeated 2 and 12 weeks after the start of the intervention, so that change in attentional and interpretative biases can be ascertained (secondary outcomes). We will also collect data on acceptability of the intervention, adherence to treatment and reasons for withdrawal from the study. We will measure acceptability by asking participants after 2 weeks: (1) please rate your experience with the CBM program: very unpleasant/ unpleasant/unsure/pleasant/very pleasant. (2) If your doctor recommends CBM treatment for you in the future, would you: refuse it/be unsure whether to do it/ be happy to do it again. Adherence will be measured automatically by the number of scheduled CBM session completed. We will consider that participants adhered to treatment if they completed at least 8 of 10 sessions. Partial and poor adherence will be defined by completion of 5-7 and less than 5 sessions, respectively. We will also ask participants who withdraw from the study to list the factors that might have contributed to their decision. Supportive collateral information will be sought from carers and treating physicians.Other study measures We will use a number of validated measures and procedures to collect demographic, lifestyle and clinical information from participants, including age, place of birth, time living in Australia, marital status, education, current and past occupations, living arrangements, religion and religion practices, weight and height, hobbies and financial concerns, smoking (never, past and current, amount and time), physical activity and alcohol consumption (standard units per day in a usual week), social support, prevalent medical conditions (diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, coronary heart disease, cerebrovascular disease, chronic respiratory diseases, arthritis and other rheumatic disorders, chronic pain, sensory impairment, cancer and others), as well as prescribed and over the counter medications (including antidepressants). The approach to data gathering will follow the same procedures that we have used successfully in other trials. [38] [39] [40] The MMSE is the most widely used screening instrument for the assessment of cognitive function worldwide. 33 Scores can range from 0 to 30, with lower scores indicating increasing cognitive impairment. The MMSE requires about 10 min to complete, and will be carried out at screening and 12 weeks. Changes of MMSE scores over 2 and 12 weeks will be additional secondary outcomes of interest of this trial. Table 1 summarises the timeline for the collection of the outcomes of the study.Sample size Data from the placebo-controlled trial of mirtazapine and sertraline indicate that people with DAD treated with placebo improved, on average, six points (SD=4) on the CSDD after 13 weeks. 15 We estimate that people treated with CBM should improve an additional three points (9 in total, SD=4). A sample size of 76 people with DAD (38 per group) would give the study 90% power to declare this difference as significant (α<5%, two-tailed). We plan to recruit 80 participants with DAD (40 randomly assigned to each treatment group) and, pessimistically, anticipate that 15% of them will be lost during the study (our retention of people with AD in our trials is higher over a period of 6 months). 41 In this case, the trial would be completed by 68 participants and would still have 87% power. We further anticipate that loss to follow-up will be minimal during the initial 2 weeks of treatment, although changes in scores may also be smaller at that point in time (three points for controls and six for intervention participants). If we lose 10% of our sample during the initial 2 weeks, primary outcome data would be available for 72 participants (36 per group) and the study would have 89% power to declare as significant such a difference between the groups. Furthermore, a sample of this size would give the study 84% power to declare as significant changes in QoL-AD scores of 1 point for controls and 4.5 points for active CBM participants over 3 months (SD=5 for both groups; positive changes indicate expected improvement). The changes in response bias (measured in milliseconds) are expected to be associated with a minimum effect size of 0.4 (Cohen's d), 26 which would require about 50 trials per participant (within group comparison). As each CBM session includes 96 trials for attention (faces) and 96 trials for interpretative bias (words), the study will have ample power to investigate this outcome.Randomisation and allocation concealment Participants will be randomly assigned to control and active CBM according to a list of random numbers generated by computer in blocks of 10. The allocation ratio will be 1:1. An independent biostatistician working at the Western Australian Centre for Health and Ageing will generate the random allocation sequence. The sequence code will then be used to automatically select the appropriate intervention for the participant (control or active CBM) from our server (ie, the id number will be linked to the randomisation code and the relevant intervention will be activated when the user id is entered on the computer). Neither the participant nor research staff will be aware of the independently controlled randomisation code. Participants will be advised about the study's aims and procedures (which will be exactly the same for all participants), but not about the details characterising the control and the active intervention. In addition, the staff member supervising the CBM sessions will not be involved in the collection of outcome data. This professional will be directed not to discuss any aspects of the intervention with other research staff. Previous trials have shown this approach is sufficient to ensure masking. 42 Group assignment codes will only be opened after the last end point of interest of the last participant in the trial is collected.Data collection and management Attentional and interpretative bias data will be collected automatically by the study computer during the assessment. In order to optimise the validity of the data collected, participants will be offered three breaks during each assessment once 25%, 50% and 75% of each session is completed. They may elect to take or override such offers of rest by asking the research officer to press the space bar, which once pressed will re-start the task. If response to a trial (faces or words) takes longer than 5 s, that trial will be disregarded in the calculation of attentional and interpretative bias (long response latencies indicate distraction rather than bias). The collection of end points will occur regardless the intervention adherence of participants. If participants become unable, or unwilling, to present to the research site for assessment, a home assessment will be offered. OPA will train research staff in the use of the CSDD and QoL-ADagreement of 0.8 or greater on weighted κ will be required before the research officer is allowed to collect end points independently (minimum of five assessments).Statistical methods We will use means and SDs to describe continuous variables with normal distribution, medians and IQRs for ordinal variables, and frequency tables for categorical variables. We will use t tests to compare the change in CSDD and QoL-AD scores between baseline and week 2. If statistical adjustments are required because of imbalance in other study measures (eg, gender distribution), we will use analysis of co-variance. We will use multilevel mixed models to analyse changes in CSDD, QoL and attentional and interpretative biases from baseline to weeks 2 and 12. We will investigate the interaction between group and time effects (statistical adjustments will be made, if necessary). This analysis is intention to treat. Multiple imputations (imputed chain equations) will be employed for the analysis of week 2 data, if necessary.Data monitoring No interim analyses are planned for this trial.Harms We do not anticipate that the procedures associated with the intervention have potential to causing clinically significant harm. Participants will be offered the opportunity of having breaks in order to minimise fatigue, although each intervention session is expected to last no more than 10 min. There will also be an opportunity for coffee and toilet breaks between intervention sessions and throughout the assessment procedures.Auditing Once a participant is randomised, the study computer will set up the study agenda for that person automaticallyadherence will be recorded, analysed and reported. A quarterly internal audit will evaluate possible violations of protocol and research schedule, as well as integrity of the study's database (including accuracy of the data recorded). Electronic records will be backed up and maintained in a secure server at the University of Western Australia. Paper records of assessments will be kept for a minimum period of 5 years following the collection of the last end point of the trial. No external auditing is planned.DISCUSSION AD is a leading cause of disability worldwide and the course of the illness is often complicated by the presence of depressive symptoms. Currently available antidepressant medications are not effective to treating DAD, so that efficacious alternatives are needed. There is strong empirical support for testing the efficacy of CBM in this population, and the design of our study has followed SPIRIT and CONSORT guidelines in order to ensure that the trial generates high quality data that address its objectives appropriately. 43 44 We have designed a CBM intervention that does not require the use of a keyboard or mouse (ie, response entails the pressing of one of two buttons of the response box) and imposes only minimal demands on the cognitive function of participants. We expect that this will increase the acceptability of and adherence to the intervention in this group of older participants.Study limitations The definition of depression in this study will be guided by the use of a validated cut-point on the CSDD rather than DSM-IV, DSM-5 or ICD-10 criteria for the diagnosis of a depressive episode. 34 We chose this approach for two reasons: (1) the DSM and ICD criteria for the diagnosis of a major depressive episode associated with AD have uncertain validity, even though provisional alternate criteria have been proposed 45 46 ; (2) pragmatic trials for the treatment of DAD have used CSDD scores of 8 or more to define clinically significant depression, with the use of the scale offering the additional advantage of measuring change in the severity of symptoms over time 15 and of allowing direct comparisons between the results of previous studies and ours. This trial will be limited to people with DAD of mild to moderate severity. Consequently, its findings will not be generalisable to older adults with severe AD. This criterion for inclusion took into account the ability of participants to offer informed consent, and the relative integrity of brain systems involved in implicit learning during the early stages of illness. CBM works through implicit learning, which might be undermined late in the course of AD. 47 The duration of the intervention in RAPID will be limited to 2 weeks, which is the time required to modify cognitive biases in adults free of cognitive impairment. 35 36 We anticipate that a similar timeline will be required to treat older adults with DAD, but cannot be certain, at this stage, that this will be the case. In addition, at this point in time, there is no direct evidence that the CBM paradigm will work as well in people with AD as it does in younger adults free of cognitive impairment. Our trial will yield such evidence, and this will allow us to ascertain if the effect (or lack of effect) of the intervention was due to the expected shifting of attentional and interpretative biases. We will monitor attentional and interpretative biases throughout the study, and this will enable us to measure the association between extinction of bias and decline in the severity of depressive symptoms. Moreover, we have included a 12-week follow-up assessment to investigate the medium term sustainability of the changes in bias and mood of participants. Finally, we acknowledge that our power calculations are based on an expectation of clinically significant improvement rather than existing preliminary data. Our predictions assume that the intervention will be associated with a relative reduction of an additional 3 out of a possible 38 points compared with controls. Consequently, this trial will be underpowered to declare as statistically significant smaller differences between the groups.Table 1 1 Timeline for the collection of outcomes for RAPID Screening Baseline Week 2 Week 12 Sociodemographic data Yes - - - Lifestyle Yes - - - Medical conditions and medications Yes - - - Daily alcohol consumption Yes - - - Confirming diagnosis of AD Yes - - - Mini-Mental State Examination Yes - Yes Yes Cornell Scale for Depression in Dementia Yes Yes Yes Yes Quality of Life AD - Yes Yes Yes Attentional and interpretative biases - Yes Yes Yes AD, Alzheimer's disease. screening_reason: 摘要中明确说明使用了随机对照试验，并且有控制组进行比较。
rejection_reason: Ineligible intervention: this RCT tests a computerised cognitive bias modification intervention for depression and quality of life in people with dementia and depression that trains participants' attentional resources to reduce repeated exposure and attention to negative stimuli.

title: Couples Counseling in Alzheimer's Disease: Additional Clinical Findings from a Novel Intervention Study
abstract: This article describes the clinical findings of a study designed to assess the benefit of counseling for couples, one of whom is in the early stage of Alzheimer's disease (AD). We previously reported our findings based on the first 12 couples that enrolled in the study. Based on the treatment of 30 additional couples, we have refined our treatment strategy to include concepts of Gestalt Therapy and Transactional Analysis and identified prevalent issues of concern to this cohort. The study design has remained as described in the earlier article (Epstein et al., 2006) , and has proven to be appropriate to meet the goals of this intervention as indicated by our clinical experience and feedback from the participating couples. Case vignettes demonstrate how to conduct the sessions so that the experience of each member of the dyad is validated, while acknowledging the differential impact of the disease on them.
text: This article describes the clinical findings of a study designed to assess the benefit of counseling for couples, one of whom is in the early stage of Alzheimer's disease (AD). We previously reported our findings based on the first 12 couples that enrolled in the study. Based on the treatment of 30 additional couples, we have refined our treatment strategy to include concepts of Gestalt Therapy and Transactional Analysis and identified prevalent issues of concern to this cohort. The study design has remained as described in the earlier article (Epstein et al., 2006) , and has proven to be appropriate to meet the goals of this intervention as indicated by our clinical experience and feedback from the participating couples. Case vignettes demonstrate how to conduct the sessions so that the experience of each member of the dyad is validated, while acknowledging the differential impact of the disease on them.
main_text: INTRODUCTION This is the second of an anticipated series of articles describing the clinical findings of a counseling intervention for couples, one of whom has been diagnosed with early-stage Alzheimer's disease (AD). In a previous article (Epstein, et al., 2006) , initial findings based on a sample of 12 couples were discussed, using two case studies. This article reflects our evolving perception of the impact of AD on the marital relationship, and a subsequent fine tuning of the therapeutic approach based on working with 30 additional couples. Our approach is to focus simultaneously on two individuals and their diverging, converging and changing emotions and needs. The clinician seeks to create a setting where both partners are invited to participate as equals, while acknowledging that one of them suffers from cognitive decline.RATIONALE As previously described (Epstein et al., 2006) , the purpose of the intervention remains to preserve the integrity of the relationship and the sense of self of each partner when one of them is diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease. "Marriage represents the single most influential and sustained relationship in the lives of many elderly spouse caregivers" (Rankin, Haut, and Since our first article on the study was published, there have been increased efforts to understand the impact of dementia on the person with the disease, as well as on the spousal relationship. Some articles looked at the quality of the marital relationship (Karney et al., 1995; Lewis, 2000) and others at the impact of the disclosure of a diagnosis of MCI or AD on the couples and their planning for the future (Husband, 2000; Blieszner et al., 2007) . Adams et al., (2008) shifted the focus from caregiver stress and burden to grief and loss, though they do not discuss the losses of the person with dementia. There have also been interventions designed to address the needs of the caregiver and treatment planning. Whitlatch (2006) developed a dyadic intervention which included spouses with dementia, but the primary focus was on care planning. We are encouraged that there appears to be a trend toward more inclusion of and attention to the psychological processes of the person with dementia (Zarit, 2004; O'Connor et al., 2007; Menne et al., 2008) . Nevertheless, these approaches differ qualitatively and conceptually from ours. We thus continue on our own journey and present the therapeutic method that emerged intuitively from our experience. We are confident that treating a couple faced with AD as a unit is an effective modality. Looking at the members of a couple as equal players in the marital drama offers an affirming view of their relationship and its capacity to embrace change.STUDY DESIGN This study employed a randomized wait-list control design. Evaluations were conducted at baseline and 2 and 4 months later. Those in the immediate treatment group received six counseling sessions within 2 months of baseline. Those in the wait-list control group were evaluated on the same schedule as the immediate treatment group and offered the same intervention beginning after the 4-month follow-up. The content of the intervention was individualized to the needs of each couple shaped by the changes in the relationship that had occurred since the onset of AD. We offered all participants brief telephone counseling upon request (ad hoc counseling) from the time of enrollment through the last follow-up assessment. Intake and follow-up assessments consisted of structured written questionnaires administered to each member of the couple separately in person to both treatment and wait-list control participants by an independent rater, who recorded their responses. The quantitative results will be the subject of another article. Here we describe the qualitative outcomes and the clinical experience of the counseling sessions.PARTICIPANTS Participants diagnosed at the Alzheimer's Disease Center (ADC) who met study criteria described below were told about the study and were invited to participate. Other couples were referred by community agencies or learned about the study from the media, flyers disseminated at conferences and workshops, or by word of mouth. A total of 42 couples enrolled in the study. We began the study by pilot testing the intervention with 3 couples. Subsequently, 39 couples were randomly assigned by lottery to the treatment or control group. Eight couples who were randomized to the wait-list control condition never received the counseling due to ill health or their disappointment at having to wait for treatment. One couple in the treatment group also did not complete the sessions due to ill health of one of the spouses. The remaining couples completed all six sessions.Participant Eligibility and Screening One member of the couple had to have received a diagnosis of AD and be in the early stage, and both needed to be willing to take part in the counseling program. An initial screening questionnaire determined probable eligibility for the study. Prior to enrollment, the couple was invited to an in-person screening session with both clinicians to confirm eligibility. Since the inception of the study, we have become more alert to possible rule-out circumstances such as medical and psychological conditions that may compromise the couple's ability to utilize this intervention and to tolerate being randomized to the delayed-entry arm of the study. These include the immediate need of treatment for psychosis, intense anxiety or severe depression, abuse, neglect, and suspicion of violence, either expressed by the couple or assessed by the clinician. In addition, we have found that couples counseling, which is based on verbal expression and exchange, may be contraindicated if the afflicted spouse suffers from moderate to severe receptive or expressive language difficulties (aphasia) or either spouse has severe uncorrected hearing loss.CHOICE OF CLINICIAN The clinician needs to be interested in counseling elderly couples confronting AD and other serious illnesses and have the necessary skills and stamina to tolerate the intense emotions and the poignancy of the couple's circumstances. Furthermore, we (the two clinicians) have found it helpful to share our own feelings and reactions to the work with each other, and to get feedback from the other professionals on our team. The curiosity and courage to explore personal responses to the work in a mutually supportive supervisory setting is analogous to the experience we ask of the couple in their therapy and enhances the ability of the clinician to perform the tasks required.CLINICAL TASKS In observing our own process we have come to realize how important it is for the clinician to: 1. Be sensitive and alert to the effects of the cultural, ethnic, and religious background of both members of the couple on their attitudes and behavior and to ask about their meaning rather than making assumptions.2. Assess whether the couple is: (a) in a crisis and requires immediate intervention, (b) needs a supportive approach, or (c) can tolerate and appreciate more insight-oriented work.3. Address the issues arising from the diagnosis of AD, each partner's individual reaction to it, and its impact on the couple. Their current coping style is considered in the context of the couple's established patterns of dealing with adversity.4. Evaluate how much of the therapeutic work should focus on educational, practical, and emotional issues.5. Consider the degree to which the couple's history together and dynamics might interfere with or support their ability to cope with the new situation.6. Be aware that issues of commitment and disappointment can be particularly acute when a couple has been together for a comparatively short time such as in a second marriage. The role of adult children of either spouse can further complicate the situation. 7. Be able to track the fluctuations in the couple's reaction to an illness, which in itself fluctuates. 8. Modulate the level of emotional intensity in the session so that the couple is not overwhelmed.9. Convey the idea that some issues can be put back 'under the rug,' or not taken out from under it, if this is what the couple chooses to do. In summary, the clinician has to communicate to the couples (many of whom are of a generation not inclined to use psychotherapy) that expressing feelings, such as anger, sadness, resentment, as well as joy and appreciation, can be helpful to both partners and will increase their ability to handle the future challenges of aging and AD. The clinician needs to be open and non-judgmental of whatever path the couple follows. One couple chose to marry to formalize their relationship after many years of being together. It is also possible that couples might choose to separate, although this has not happened in our study sample.THEORETICAL UNDERPINNINGS Our clinical approach is present-oriented and addresses long-established dynamics only when they interfere with mastering current challenges. In addition to our backgrounds in psychodynamic and Gestalt therapies, we have found theoretical support and useful conceptual formulations from Transactional Analysis (Harris, 1969; Ernst, 1971; Berne, 1961) and from writings by Winnicott (1971) . Transactional analysis (TA), is based on the theory that there are four existential life positions, and identifies them as, "I am not ok, you're ok; I am not ok, you're not ok; I am ok, you're not ok; I am ok, you're ok" (Ernst, 1971) . Transactional analysis further posits that people under stress tend to blame others for their distress. Depending on life circumstances, individuals will use one of these positions as a starting point from which they interact with others or may return temporarily to what Berne calls the "child ego state," replaying scenes from their childhood when they lack the resources to maintain an adult ego state. Identifying where, in Ernst's "Okay corral," the members of the couple were located helped structure the work. Put simply, the goal of the treatment for the couple is to arrive at the "We're both okay" position. Referring to these formulations can bring the couples' present life positions into the open, thereby encouraging their examination and the reduction of blame and negative judgment. This approach avoids the common pitfall of assigning the "one down" role of identified patient to the person with dementia and the "one up" positioning of the well spouse, which would be consistent with a medical model. Instead we are guided by the notion: 'No one is to blame; what matters is how we go forward and figure things out.' We discovered that the couples could shift toward mutual acceptance when a safe environment, much like the holding environment described by Winnicott, was created in the session. Winnicott's definition of psychotherapy as an activity where two (or three) people play together is a useful construct. According to Winnicott (1971) , it is the task of the clinician to help create the environment where this play is possible. Looking at what one does, at how one feels, without immediately judging it as good/bad/right/ wrong was initially unfamiliar to the couples but opened up a space for curiosity and engagement, which is the playfulness described by Winnicott. In this 'blame free' zone the couple could 'play with' the issues they were confronting, and feelings could be examined, moved around, looked at from different angles. In our work, the clinician shifts back and forth between the members of the couple, holding the reality of each with equal attention, offering them the opportunity to consider the validity of each other's experience without judgment. The clinician has to be mindful of the nonafflicted spouse's tendency to seek an alliance with the clinician in which the ill spouse becomes the object of scrutiny. Consequently, the well spouse may be taken aback in the first session, while the ill partner lights up and sits a little straighter as a result of the shift in perspective.CLINICAL CASE EXAMPLES Case vignettes will illustrate our approach and describe common reaction patterns of couples confronted with AD (the couples' names have been changed as have elements of the scenarios to protect confidentiality).Previously Unexpressed Resentments The following is an example of the shifts that can take place in a relationship when the 'unspeakable' can be spoken, with the encouragement and support of the clinician. The counseling invites both spouses to give up the irrational and unrealistic expectations of the idealized perfect union and to live more realistically together after having expressed their truths to each other. The couple is no longer stuck in their old standoff, but may be able to adapt more flexibly to the new situation. When the reality of the progressive nature of the illness seeps into the awareness of the well spouse, feelings of resentment may emerge because any hopes for future satisfaction of deferred needs are dashed. The well spouse becomes aware that relying on the sustaining comfort of looking forward to better times and a 'better' spouse who turns from frog to prince and becomes the ideal companion who is appreciative and emotionally available, is no longer realistic, and confronts the realization that this cannot happen, because of the relentless progression of the illness. Now the well spouse has to accept responsibility for the decisions and choices that brought him or her to this place. The expected compensations no longer assuage the suppressed needs and desires. Regrets about paths not taken that might have rescued the spouse from the current situation and are no longer imaginable, such as divorce ("I should have left after his/her affair"), unexpressed resentments, or opportunities not pursued, can come roiling forth in rage, hatred, or depression. Case vignette 1-The Lees-This couple has been married for 40 years, has two daughters, and is financially comfortable. Mrs. Lee had been an actress who gave up her career aspirations to get married. She felt that she was plain looking and could not afford to turn down what she feared would be her only marriage proposal. Mr. Lee had been a business man. He is very aware of his failing memory, and writes lists to navigate through daily life. In their first session the couple appeared 'dug in,' like two fighters in their corners in the ring, mostly too tired to move or fight much, but also unwilling to leave their long held positions, the wife's of hurt and disappointment and the husband's of ignoring her feelings. Mr. Lee came to the sessions with a list of his concerns, which dealt mostly with his wife's physical health, her weight, her drinking and her general passivity. He wanted her to exercise and stay healthy so that she could take care of him and "push my wheelchair." Mrs. Lee acknowledged that she resented his demands, and declared she had no intention of exercising, losing weight, or quitting drinking. The clinician noticed that the couple never looked at each other during the first two sessions and was struck by a sense of this being 'pay back time,' but what for was unclear. In the third session the clinician asked Mr. Lee if he knew what his wife was angry about. He shrugged his shoulders. Clinician: "Have you ever thought of asking? You could ask right now". Mr. L.: "There are a few things I know she was not happy about …" (trails off) Clinician: "You want to list them yourself or ask her right now?" (totally matter of fact): "Me? Of course not!" Clinician: "So, what we have here are two people who tell each other that they would not change each other's diapers, which might have to be dealt with in the future, but right now you are both diaper free, right?" (Both smile and nod)… Clinician (to Mr. L.): "Did you know that Elise never felt loved by you?" Mr. L.: "I think I love her, maybe not enough, I never felt loved by my mother." (Wife interjects: "They had a terrible relationship."), "and I am used to Elise, her quirks, and she is really smart and we enjoy the theater … and I trust her …" Clinician (to Mrs. L.): "How does that sound to you?" She shrugs and blows her nose…. At the next session the Lees appeared as if a weight has been lifted from each of them, looking at ease with each other. Mrs. L. started by saying that Willie is the only person she can be herself with, that she is a very private person, uncomfortable with most people, but not with him. This was the first time she spoke of herself as an individual with her own personality and not only as an unloved wife. Having expressed her pain, she appeared to have gained access to other feelings and was able to see some benefit in having Willie as a partner. In the space that was created by the reduction in acrimony, Elise and Willie spent the final two sessions planning for the future.Ill Spouse's Denial that Behavior Could be Disease-Related Although study participants initially acknowledge the diagnosis of AD, it is common that specific instances of forgetting or confusion are denied or rationalized. The well spouse then may press the ill spouse to admit the lapses, provoking a defensive response. This is illustrated in the following vignette: Case vignette 2-The Allens-Mr. and Mrs. A. have been married for 10 years. It is the second marriage for both. One of the pleasures they share is going on ocean cruises. Mrs. A. exhibited characteristic symptoms of early stage AD, such as difficulty organizing and planning, and finding her way in new surroundings. Shortly before entering the study they had returned from a lengthy cruise and were considering future trips. The following is an exchange that occurred as Mr. A. expressed his concerns about his wife's ability to find her way around the ship. At this point the couple faces a task together. For the moment, they are on the same page, rather than each looking to justify and to defend his or her view. The clinician's response joins the couple together in their mutual distress. The clinician speaks the reality that each seeks to deny or avoid, which grounds the work by taking it out of the blaming game and releasing the fears and anxiety it conceals.Well Spouse's Denial that a Behavior may be Disease-Related Although less common, the well spouse may deny that some behaviors of the ill spouse are symptoms of AD. The following vignette illustrates this: Case vignette 3-The Greens-Mr. and Mrs. G. have been married for 5 years. Mrs. G. works part time in sales and Mr. G., the person with dementia, sells candy at a kiosk in an office building. Mrs. G. declares that she will not tolerate her husband's failure to support her need to maintain an active life apart from him and his fearful reactions when she is a few minutes late. She insists that he can change his reaction and behavior and threatens to leave if he fails to do so. Mrs. G. does not accept that these are additional symptoms of the illness that go beyond poor memory, such as anxiety, fearfulness, confusion, and lack of emotional containment and responsiveness to her needs. Instead, she surmises that he uses the illness to cover up the fact that he never liked her to go out without him. This disagreement has a long history, but in the past Mr. G. was apparently better able to control his reactions, and Mrs. G. continues to believe that he should be able to do so now. Mrs. G. was annoyed with some of her husband's behaviors even before they married but had assumed that she would be able to change them after they married. She now struggles not to give up her belief that she can turn him into the husband she wants him to be.Mrs. G.: "We had a terrible week. On Monday I came home a few minutes late and there he was, yelling." Mr. G.: "But hon, I don't yell. Did I really yell?" Mrs. G.: "You went like this." (Spreading her arms to imitate his gestures, shouting) "'Where were you?' And you had called all over to find out where I was, and I was just two minutes late." (Mr. G. sits and stares. The clinician looks at him and he makes a gesture of 'What can I do?') Clinician to Mr. G.: "Well it sounds to me like you were scared. You didn't know where your wife was." Mrs. G. interrupts: "He knew, he knew, I wrote him a note when I would be home." Clinician: "Did you have the note when you got worried?" (Mr. G. nods his assent.) Clinician: "It sounds like the two minutes were too long for you." Mrs. G.: "His kids tell me that he was always like that. Maybe it has something to do with losing his first two wives and his mother." Clinician: "That sounds like a lot of losses. What about your mother?" Mr. G.: "I was seven when she died." Clinician: "This is so touching. You might feel like a seven-year-old when you watch the clock, waiting for your wife to come home and not being sure that she will return." Mr. G. (very animated): "She might be in an accident, in the hospital, under a bus. And in my head I know I am overreacting, but down here (points to his chest) I can't help it …." Mrs. G.: "He's not worried for me. He's worried for himself." Clinician (turns to Mrs. G.): "Well, if you feel like a seven-year-old, of course you are worried for yourself, because you cannot survive without an adult. …." (Silence, they both look at each other). Mrs. G.: "In my head I understand he might feel like the seven-year-old, but here I don't (pointing to her chest), I get so angry and just want to run." Clinician: "So you have that in common; you under-stand each other in your heads, but your guts give you a different message and you don't feel compassion for each other…." When the distressing behavior of the person with dementia is familiar, in that it is an exaggeration of a behavior that occurred prior to the illness, it is more difficult for the well spouse to accept it as a symptom of AD. In the above vignette the well spouse had always experienced her husband's tendency to insist on punctuality as an attempt to control her, and had hoped that her threats to leave him would motivate him to change. Now she might need to consider that her husband's behavior is and may always have been an expression of what TA calls a "child ego state" that is now exacerbated by his illness. Her husband's fearful behavior may similarly trigger Mrs. G's own regression, which Berne calls contamination of the "adult ego state," preventing her from recognizing the impact of the illness and leaving her anchored to her anger and denial. When a client is not able to put his or her reactions into the "play space" and to consider their multiple meanings and functions, the clinician can only observe to the couple how important it now seems for them to hold on to their beliefs and to suggest that they may not always feel this way. Whether a belief functions as a protection or a weapon, it will not be relinquished without a satisfactory alternative. Our research has shown that counseling can alter a caregiver's negative appraisal of a dementia-related behavior (Mittelman, et al., 2004) . Negative appraisal on the part of the caregiver can render the person with dementia at higher risk for nursing home placement (Mittelman, et al., 2006) . However, the intention is not just to shift the appraisal from negative to positive, but to a more realistic one that can be a resource for coping (McNulty 2008) . Coming Through the Fog-I Am Still Here, Sometimes In the supportive atmosphere of the sessions the ill spouse can often become engaged in dialogue much as he/she had prior to the illness. The ill spouse is energized and mobilizes still available inner resources to respond to the invitation to be a full participant in the sessions. This experience can be challenging to the well spouse who has to accommodate to the "continuation or periodic reappearance of the 'former self' of the person with dementia" (Robinson et al., 2005) , and to shift from the more emotionally removed caregiver role back to that of spouse. In the following vignette we see such a resurfacing. Case vignette 4-The Grays-Mr. and Mrs. Gray have been married for over 20 years in second marriages for both. The husband, who has dementia, is 93 years old, but looks like 78. Mrs. Gr. is 78, and also looks much younger than her age. Mrs. Gr. starts the final session: "Things are going so much better…." Clinician: "How come?" Mr. Gr.: "Well, it's you, you did it." (pointing to the clinician). (Mrs. Gr. nods in agreement). Clinician: "What did I do?" This last session renewed this couple's desire to be more caring and present with each other and motivated the well spouse to encourage her husband to make an effort and connect with his 'old self' when possible.The Role of Humor In the previous vignette the wife noted that the husband no longer uses "nasty humor," a device which is frequently employed by husbands with AD, usually to the distress of the wife. Rarely is the humor and joking experienced as funny, but rather as a persistent and dissonant note that becomes a further thorn in the side of the wife. The clinician also found herself the object of the husband's 'humorous' barbs, but she understood that they had multiple functions-as a communication tool, which can be used as a weapon, but also as a means to lighten uncomfortable and embarrassing situations. It is interesting to note that among the couples in our study, we did not see wives with dementia utilize this coping strategy. The following vignette, where the wife has lost her benevolent attitude toward her husband's struggle to feel good about himself, is another example of the changing dynamic in a marriage She now openly expresses her disgust and impatience with his humorous attempts to assert a "one up" position. Case vignette 5-The Bergers-The Bs., a couple married for 5 years in a second marriage for both, could be heard in the hallway before entering the office. Mrs. B. was yelling at her husband, "Why don't you shut up once in a while! That was not funny and you embarrassed me and that woman! She did not want to talk to you!" In response, Mr. B. looked a little sheepish, but lightened up the minute he saw the clinician, greeting her with a big smile and a loud hello. "I try to please everybody, make things better, and she only yells at me." Clinician: "It must be hurtful to you too then, you're trying so hard and your wife only feels hurt and angry. Actually, you are both hurt and angry." The remainder of the session was spent exploring the issue of disappointment, which loomed large with this couple. Mrs. B. admitted that she never found her husband's jokes funny, but grinned and bore them since he had other wonderful qualities. Again, this is a common feature with many married couples: The wife smiles and rolls her eyes while the husband is joking with the receptionist, waitress, etc. Mr. B., in turn, senses that his wife does not find his jokes funny (anymore) and increases his efforts, unable to change the script. The clinician needs to be mindful not to identify with the wife's exasperation and to bring this dynamic to the couple's attention. While most attempts by husbands with early AD to utilize humor have not had the desired outcome, we had one striking example in which the husband's sense of humor continues to enrich the couple's connection. Having known each other for over 30 years, Mr. Brown finally proposed to marry his partner when he realized that her ability to take care of him would be hampered by her lack of legal status. Mr. Brown's sophisticated, wry, and offbeat sense of humor remains largely intact in spite of his condition. His type of humor has been characterized by Stocking and Zillmann (1976) , as absurd, typified by word plays and clowning. Mrs. Brown enjoys her husband's humor, and is not put off by its sometimes unpredictable turns. For instance, at a visit to the urologist, as the doctor tried gently to prepare him for an upcoming rectal exam, Mr. Brown said, "I know what to do" and opened his mouth wide. The doctor stared at him, perplexed. The wife explained her husband's joke by adding, "You should see what he does at the dentist," and all laughed. On another occasion, Mrs. Brown, by chance, met her husband with his aide on the bus (he was coming from a day program) and offered her seat to him, since his balance had become quite unsteady. He responded in a loud voice, "Oh thank you so much, will you marry me?" The whole bus fell quiet. The passengers at first did not know how to react, then broke into laughter. Unlike the unsuccessful use of humor to minimize stress and or to maintain status, Mr. Brown does not put others down. Instead he invites others to play along.CONCLUSION Our study has shown that it is possible and worthwhile to offer counseling to couples when one member is in the early stage of AD, and that our clinical approach is suitable for both members of the dyad. Six sessions proved sufficient to shift the couples into a mode in which they could face the future together more optimistically and collaboratively. When each member of the couple is able to take the other's behavior less personally, and assume an accepting, nonjudgmental, non blaming attitude, the relationship stands on a firmer footing. All couples could probably benefit from developing these relationship muscles, but those coping with a disability may derive even greater benefit from interventions such as this one. Alzheimer's disease is a progressive chronic disease, which makes it difficult to predict the long term benefits of this intervention. Nevertheless, we feel it is important to recognize and support the ongoing connection between the members of the couple. It is our hope that our intervention will be replicated and expanded upon in the future.Mr. L.: "Okay, so what are you angry about?" (Mrs. L. had started to cry while this exchange was going on.) Mrs. L.: "I don't know what this is supposed to do. There is nothing that can be done now and I wouldn't know where to start …." Mr. L.: "See?" Clinician to Mrs. L. "Hold on for a minute here. Just start somewhere and we'll take it step by step (turns to Mr. L). "Right?" (He nods bravely) Mrs. L.: "Well, he knows." (Cries harder) "I am not even angry about your affair and I knew you didn't come back because you loved me. You came back because you missed the kids, having a family. It sounds like I am competing with my own child but I am not! What I cannot forget is what you said when Barbara was born, 'Until now I did not know what love was.' I should have left right then instead of living with someone who could or would not love me, but I was too scared," (blows her nose) "and now you only care about me so that I can take care of you and I won't do it!" Clinician: "Do what exactly?" Mrs. L.: "Well, you heard him, push him in the wheel-chair; change his diapers …." Clinician: "Wow! Hold it right here." (turns to husband). "You have mentioned that you want your wife to be fit enough to take care of you, right?" (He nods.) "So what would you do if she can't move around anymore and you would have to push her in a wheelchair or change her diapers? Would you do that?" Mr. L.:Your husband may be exaggerating or overly protective and you might be forgetting some details. What we are trying to figure out is how the two of you can deal with this." Mr. A.: "I am not sure that we can continue to go on these cruises since my wife can't be trusted to find her way back to our cabin and I don't want to always be worried about her." Mrs. A.: "What are you talking about? I never had any problem. I always found my way back." Mr. A.: "But you had to ask someone." Mrs. A.: "No I didn't." Mr. A.: "Yes you did." Clinician: "Let's stop for a moment. Let's figure out what is going on." (Clinician leans forward and looks at the couple with curiosity. This slows down the process.) Clinician (to Mrs. A.): "This must be so upsetting. You sound like you feel accused" … Clinician (to Mr. A.): "You must be so frustrated to hear her denying what you say when you feel that you are right. Let's look at this again." Clinician (to Mrs. A.): "You both agreed to meet with me so we can look at how your memory problems affect how the two of you are with each other." Mrs. A.: "But they are not as bad as he says." Clinician: "
